Assign personnel to monitor collection and remittance of FSIF.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the:
Acting City Accountant and the General Services Officer to:
Coordinate and reconcile their records with the PS-DBM;
Apply the excess advances to succeeding APRs and/or immediately request for a refund; and
Strictly adhere to the COA Circular No. 2016-005 particularly on the conduct of regular and periodic reconciliation of the account balances.
Acting City Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries in the books based on the results of the reconciliation.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:
City Health Officer to closely coordinate with the Chief of Hospital to secure necessary data and information to facilitate the establishment of the complete list or breakdown of the professional fees due the concerned health care personnel; and
The Acting City Accountant and City Health Officer to distribute the long outstanding amount of professional fees totaling P9,006,281.61 to 
health care personnel in compliance with DOH AO No. 2016-033 and Section 34-A of RA No. 10606, and PhilHealth Circular No. 0035, s. 2013.

We recommended that the LCE require the:
Responsible offices/end users to facilitate the implementation of the three projects and programs for the funds received from DBM, DOH and TESDA, to attain the ultimate purpose of the fund transferred; and
Acting City Accountant to immediately return the excess/unexpended balances of fund transfer to the respective SAs in compliance with COA Circular No. 94-013.

We recommended that the LCE direct the General Services Officer to ensure the timely submission of the RPCI to the Office of the City Auditor in accordance with Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Acting City Accountant and the General Services Officer to facilitate the maintenance of the SLCs for each commodity/stock, and SCs for supplies, respectively, in compliance with Section 114 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Acting City Health Officer to:
Regularly submit to the CAO the report on PhilHealth claims duly supported with complete list of member patients containing relevant information pertaining to the claims, for the appropriate recording in the books of accounts; and
Submit to the CAO the Notification Letters for Denied and RTH claims to serve as basis in derecognizing or deducting the same from the recorded receivables from PhilHealth.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the PhilHealth Committee of the NCH and the Chief of Hospital to:
Make representation with the SP for the amendment of the inconsistent provision of Section 4 of City Ordinance No. 2023-13, to harmonize the same with the existing rules and regulations issued by PhilHealth; and
Expedite the completion of the IRR in order to provide clear guidelines 
for the efficient and effective implementation of City Ordinance 
No. 2023-13.

We recommended that Management require the City Treasurer’s Office (CTO) to regularly remit the FSIF to the BFP not later than the two (2) days prescribed period after the transaction is made in compliance with Section 11(b), Rule IX of the IRR of RA No. 11032.

We recommended that:
The LCE designate permanent employees as Collecting Officers, and shall be properly bonded in compliance with Section 65 of the GAAM, Volume I, Section 101 of PD No. 1445, Section 2.1 of the Treasury Order No. 01-95;
The City Treasurer stop from deputizing JO personnel as collecting officers; and
The Human Resource Development Officer facilitates the recruitment and hiring of qualified applicants to augment the limited manpower resources of the CTO.

We recommended that the LCE direct the General Services Officer to:
Coordinate with the Acting City Accountant for the identification of the remaining uninsured properties, and facilitate the inclusion of the said assets to the PIF for the required insurance of the same to the GSIS; and
Ensure that all future acquisitions of insurable properties and other assets are included in the PIF to be submitted to the GSIS for insurance.

We recommended that the LCE direct the General Services Officer to submit the PIF, along with relevant documents to COA SA/ATL and the GSIS not later than April 30 of each year, in compliance with the pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2018-002.

We recommended that the LCE direct the OLBO to:
Ensure the full and timely implementation of all programmed PPAs by setting realistic timelines for its execution; and
Maximize the utilization of the 15 % share on income derived from building permit fees and other charges, for the improvement and enhancement of its operations and facilities.

We recommended that the LCE direct the CTO in coordination with the OLBO to make representation with the SP to secure an authority to open a separate bank account for the OLBO’s 15% share on income derived from building permit fees and other charges in compliance with Section 2.1 of DPWH-DILG JMC No. 1.

We recommended the LCE require the GFPS to ensure the submission of the DILG-approved GPB together with the Certificate of Endorsement to the Audit Team within five working days upon receipt of endorsement pursuant to Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001.

We recommended that the LCE to require the GFPS to ensure the submission of the GAD AR based on the DILG endorsed GPB together with the required reports to the Office of the City Auditor within five working days from the end of January of the preceding year in compliance with to Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the GFPS to:
Observe the proper planning, monitoring, and evaluation on the implementation of GAD-related PPAs to maximize the utilization of the GAD funds and attain the objectives for which the fund was established;
Provide the breakdown of the budget and expenditure for each PPAs to support the actual GAD cost or expenditure and to properly classify the implemented PPAs; and
Ensure that the GAD AR fully represents the City’s actual accomplishments concerning GAD fund utilization.

We recommended that the LCE direct the GFPS and the CAO to reconcile their records and make the necessary adjustments to accurately reflect the GAD Fund utilization, and henceforth, ensure coordination and regular reconciliation of their records.

We recommended that the LCE to:
Reconstitute and strengthen the existing GFPS by replacing reassigned and retired members, excluding DILG Navotas City Director as a member GFPS-Executive Committee, and appointing members of GFPS Technical Working Group (TWG); and
Ensure compliance with Item 4.1 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 on the creation of the GFPS.

We recommended that the LCE require the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (CDRRMO) to fully implement all DRRM PPAs on the scheduled timelines, and to closely monitor its completion to ensure the optimal utilization of the DRRM Funds.

We recommended that the LCE require the CDRRMO to prepare and update annually the LDRRMFIP the using the format prescribed in COA Circular 
No. 2012-002, and incorporate the 30% lump-sum allocation for the QRF, the PPAs to be funded from the 70% allocation for disaster mitigation, prevention, and preparedness, and any unexpended LDRRMF from previous years.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:
Acting City Accountant to immediately establish and maintain a separate RAAO for QRF, MOOE and CO for the City’s DRRM Funds in compliance with Item No.5.1.4 of COA Circular 2012-002 to ensure that each transaction is accounted for according to its intended purpose and warrant the efficient and effective monitoring of its utilization and balances; and
Acting City Accountant and Acting City Budget Officer to ensure accurate classification and timely recording of all related financial transactions.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the CDRRMO to prepare the RRUCD for cash donations received using the prescribed format and to submit the same to the OCD, copy furnished the Office of the City Auditor pursuant to COA Circular No. 2014-002.

We recommended that the LCE direct the CDRRMO to ensure the submission of the RSUDRRMF to the Office of the City Auditor through the LDRRMC and the LDC on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.

We recommended that the LCE direct the NCCPC to carefully plan and timely execute the implementation of all designed PPAs to ensure its completion and the attainment of the Council’s ultimate objectives towards protecting the rights and welfare of the children in conflict with the law.

We recommended that the Management to increase the allocation of funds in the City’s annual budget for the implementation of identified climate change PPAs pursuant to the provisions of Sections 14 and 18 of RA 
No. 9729 and Item 4.1 of the DBM-CCC-DILG JMC No. 2015-01, to ensure the achievement of an enhanced adaptive capacity to battle the adverse effects of climate change.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Management direct the CAO to ensure that premium contributions and loan amortizations deducted and withheld from City officials and employees are remitted in full and within the prescribed period to the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth pursuant to the applicable laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Acting City Accountant to:
Facilitate the remittance in full of all taxes withheld (current and prior years’) to the BIR; and
Strengthen internal control by regularly conducting review and reconciliation between the withholding tax records and remittance schedules to avoid incurrence of under/over remittance of taxes withheld to the BIR.

We recommended that the Management:

Direct the Inventory Committee to undertake and complete the physical count of all PPE items, and reconcile the discrepancies in accordance with Chapter V of the handbook and Section 491 of the GAAM, Volume I; and


Avail the one-time cleansing of the PPE provided in Commission on Audit (COA) Circular No. 2020-006, by adhering to the Guidelines and Procedures in the Conduct of Physical Count of PPE, Recognition of PPE Items Found at the Station, and Disposition of Non-existing/ Missing PPE Items, for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances of Government Agencies.
observations.managementAction:

No. 2020-006, which was duly approved by the COA on November 18, 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the City’s Solid Waste Management Board and Community Environment and Natural Resources Office (CENRO):

Monitor the MRF situated in barangays and continue to provide necessary support to component barangays either financially, technically or in any other manner deemed necessary to achieve the waste diversion goal set by the City for the coming years; and



Adopt measures to promote and ensure the viability and effective implementation of RA 
No. 9003 in the City’s component barangays by establishing MRF in every Barangay, or cluster with other Barangays, if they are unable to do so because of logistical requirements, to ensure recovery of recyclable and compostable materials and maximize the benefits derived therefrom.

We further recommended that the City Mayor direct the CENRO Officer to involve closely the barangay officials in solid waste management by activating the Solid Waste Management Committee in every barangay for the strict enforcement of waste segregation at source; and intensify information campaign on solid waste management.
observations.managementAction:
The CENRO constantly monitored the existing MRFs of the seven barangays to ensure its functionality. Likewise, the City partnered with the Philippine Red Cross for the upgrading of the electrical connection of the City’s Materials Recovery Facility in order to operationalize the existing solid waste processing equipment. Moreover, the Acting CENRO Chief designated two personnel as MRF Monitoring Officers.

To promote and ensure the viability and effective implementation of RA 9003, the CENRO entered into a Memorandum of Understanding with Green Antz Builders Inc. for the collection of all types of clean and dry plastic wastes from all schools of Navotas to be upcycled into construction bricks as an additional LGU waste diversion initiative. 

Further, for the 13 barangays with no MRF, a substitute scheme called Materials Recovery System was established wherein the accredited junkshop accommodate all the recyclable materials segregated at source.
By virtue of Executive Order No. JRT-031, s. 2024, the City Solid Waste Management Board (CSWMB) was reorganized to include all 18 Barangay Chairpersons to actively participate in the enforcement of waste segregation. 

Likewise, Mother Earth Foundation capacitated and assisted the 18 barangays in conducting a door-to-door SWM Information, Education and Communication (IEC) campaign on August 2 to September 9, 2024 covering a total of 7,417 households to teach proper at-source segregation and waste management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Finance Committee and Director of Navotas City Hospital to use the revenue targets in the real property taxes to further intensify collections therefrom. Avail of administrative and judicial remedies in enforcing payments particularly in tax delinquencies.
observations.managementAction:
The target RPT for 
CY 2022 was not achieved due to the effects of the COVID-19 pandemic on the preceding years which significantly reduced the paying capacities of the people. 

Likewise, various laws and national directives on the reduction of taxes and other impositions coupled with the Navotas City general pandemic amnesty program under City Ordinance No. 2021-62 effective 01 January to 30 June 2022, influenced the outcome of RPT collection. | 
 | Non-codification of the City's 2017 Revenue Code resulted in 45 loose ordinances not included in the newly approved Revenue Code and ambiguous provisions on taxes, fees, and charges to facilitate ready reference/clearer interpretation to users or implementers.


We recommended that Management:

Consider the codification of the revenue, rules, and regulations to be included in the newly enacted Revenue Code in December 2017. This will provide a ready reference for the implementers and taxpayers. We further suggested that Management coordinate with the Sangguniang Panlungsod to come up with a codified or consolidated Revenue Code; and




Coordinate with the Sangguniang Panlungsod to revisit the provisions of the Revenue Code to come up with amendments that would give the clearer interpretation of revenue provisions.
observations.reason:
The Sangguniang Panlungsod Secretariat will act on the request of management and will discuss with the City Council the codification of revenue rules and regulations. SP Secretariat will request for budget for the printing of codified revenue code as included in the 2023 Annual Report (Plan for 2024).

Consideration shall be made on the adjustment of tax rates pursuant to Sec. 191 (Authority of the LGUs to Adjust Tax Rates) of the Local Government Code. It is noteworthy to mention that the SP Secretariat already arranged the supplemental ordinances and resolutions in chronological order. | Task overloading of SP Secretariat staff.

Unavailability of software and budget allocation for the equipment needed in the digitization of codified ordinances.







SP Secretariat will discuss with the City Council (Committee on Ways and Means), coordinate with the City Treasurer, present records of the Revenue Code and amendments that are possible for codification and printing.

Monitor the stocks to avoid overstocking of food supplies that may result in spoilage of food and wastage.

Direct the Human Resource Development Officer to fill the vacant positions in the Office of the City Treasurer so that there are enough collecting officers.

Plan and execute timely the implementation of the programs/ projects/activities and exert efforts to fully utilize the funds for intended PPAs to ensure the optimum utilization of the NCCPC Fund; and

We further recommended that the City Mayor direct the CENRO Officer to involve closely the barangay officials in solid waste management by activating the Solid Waste Management Committee in every barangay for the strict enforcement of waste segregation at source; and intensify information campaign on solid waste management.

Fully comply with the provisions of RA No. 9729 and DBM, CCC, and DILG JMC No. 2015-01.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that the Management ensure that contributions are remitted on time and in full within the prescribed pursuant to the applicable regulations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended for the CAO and CTO to:
Account for the variances noted in the RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred RPT/SET between the CAO books of accounts and the Delinquency Listing of Real Property Taxes record of the CTO; and
Reconcile the variances and reflect/adjust accordingly the correct balances of the RPT/SET Receivables and the Deferred RPT/SET.

We recommended  that the City through the City Assessor:
Secure sworn declarations of real property from the owners/administrators of industrial, commercial and residential properties that were listed as exempted from taxes as required in Section 202 of the LGC for the proper determination of their assessed values and appropriate application of assessment levels;
Require the submission/filing of sufficient documentary evidence from the owners/administrators who claimed tax exemption for property as required under Section 206 of the LGC. If the required proof has not been submitted, the property should be listed as taxable in the assessment roll;
Re-assess the property with market values of P175,000.00 and below that were previously exempted from the RPT and impose the corresponding tax level and tax rate for RPT and SET; and
Undertake a general revision of assessment and property classification as provided in Sections 219 and 220 of the LGC.

We recommended that:
The Inventory Committee reconcile the results of the inventory count with the property and accounting records after the conduct of the physical count. The inventory listing of equipment should be checked with the property cards maintained by the CGSO as against the equipment ledger cards maintained by the CAO and total therefore shall be compared with those in the general ledger;
The CAO –
Continue validation of the reconciling items in the PPE account and accordingly prepare adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts;
Sort/present the proper details of PPE according to its nature and capitalize additional cost of rehabilitation, repairs and improvements to the specific property and deduct the cost for the portion of the property which was already dropped or demolished; and
Exert extra effort to determine the details of property items without description/breakdown and lumped as beginning balances and all PPEs without details/breakdown.
The City General Service Officer include only in the RPCPPE the property items actually counted, seen and found during the conduct of the physical count. Significant discrepancies noted during the count should be immediately investigated and explained, and reconciled with the CAO.

We recommended that:
The CAO to –
Request for copies of the certificates on investments recorded in the books and henceforth, to retain copies or proofs of ownership at all times as part of the City’s permanent file; and
Confirm the investments/guaranty deposits with the Investee/Company concerned and complete the details in the SL, and thereafter, adjust/correct those that were already derecognized or applied in previous payments.
The CTO to secure at all times, all Investment certificates, like time deposits, treasury bills and stock certificates, which shall be inventoried showing their serial numbers, amounts and type of investments for the purpose of   turn over or transfer of accountability per Sections 116 and 117 of the Local Treasury Operations Manual.

We recommended that the CAO:
Exert efforts to locate the deliquent borrowers who availed of the long outstanding livelihood loans assistance by sending demand letters, or refer the matter to the City Legal Office for any appropriate legal action that may be taken;
Adjust the entries made for the amount of utility expenses consumed (electric and water bills) paid by the contractors during the completion of the projects/programs in the City of Navotas; and
Reclassify into the proper accounts the noted misclassified accounts in the books.

We recommended that the CAO:
Obtain from the bank and the CTO all the necessary documents needed to support the identified reconciling items; and
Record the identified reconciling items in the books for fair presentation of the balances of Cash in Bank-Local Currency - Current Account, Hospital Fees and Due to Officers and Employees.

We recommended that:
The GSO –
Prepare the PIF listing of all the insurable properties and other assets base on the RPCPPE, with the complete required data to ensure that all insurable properties of the City are covered with insurance;
Exclude unserviceable properties in the PIF; and
Submit the consolidated PIF to the Office of the Auditor and the GIF of the GSIS not later than April 30 of each  year.
The CAD to correct the misclassified accounts in the accounting records to present a reliable financial statements.

We recommended that the Management:
Direct the BAC to consolidate meticulously and judiciously the PPMPs from various units, to be integrated into a workable APP, including the submission of APP-CSE for the common-use supplies and equipment as prescribed in GPPB Circular 08-2015 and GPPB Resolution 
No. 20-2015;
Make use of the APP in the determination of the proper mode of procurement that will be advantageous to the City and to avoid purchasing supplies/materials/equipment on a per department/office/ end-user unit basis; and
Require the concerned department/office to regularly update their PPMPs and the BAC to accordingly update the APP once every six months to reflect the necessary revision for the approval of the Local Chief Executive (LCE).

We recommended that Management:
Revisit the organizational structure of the CDRRMC to ensure that all offices/departments that are required to be members thereof as provided in RA No. 10121 are represented; and
Consider the requirements and standards for GAD mainstreaming, as well as programs and projects for risk areas related to veterinary services in the updating of the City’s annual DRRM Plan.

We recommended that the CDRRMC  prepare  the LDRRMF Investment Plan annually to support the utilization of the Trust Fund, duly approved by the LDRRM Council and the LCE as provided in COA Circular 
No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the City Accountant:
Reconcile the information in the confirmation letter of DPWH against the recorded programs, amount and settlements in the books, submit liquidations reports, if any; and
Return the unutilized/unimplemented amount to the DPWH, if found appropriate.

We recommended that:
The City Engineering Office to –
Effect a system to properly identify and/or account each streetlight in order that such as date of installation, contractor/supplier, schedule of repairs/maintenance and the like are easily accessible; and
Conduct regular inspection as well as periodic repairs and maintenance, in order that the Road Lighting infrastructure may provide better and safer night-time environment on the roads, streets and parks of the City and that socio-economic benefits thereon may be derived.
The General Services Office to maintain the necessary property cards for monitoring, showing the historical records of each infrastructure projects-streetlights, which include among others the complete description, exact locations and other necessary details, considering that projects funded from the 20% Development Fund partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures.

We recommended that the City School Board:
Refund the amount of P1,492,650.00 for the payment of seminars/trainings attended by school officials/teachers which are not allowed to be charged against the SEF;
Refrain from charging expenses against the SEF unless authorized by the law, rules and regulations; and
Henceforth, ensure that PPAs to be financed by the SEF are priority programs and authorized projects identified in relevant law, rules and regulations to benefit the operation and maintenance of public elementary and secondary schools.

We recommended that the City HRDO initiate the conversion of the position classifications filled by casual employees in accordance with the new administrative classes as required by DBM Circular No. 2004-3 dated March 6, 2004.

We recommended that the City revisit the guidelines being adopted and make enhancement/improvements thereon that would result to a fair assessment or evaluation in the grant of compensation/wages and step increment to its non-permanent personnel.

We recommended that the City’s GFP System:
Take initial action for the formulation and issuance of the Rules and Regulations implementing the City’s GAD Code as provided under Section  98, Article II of City Ordinance No. 2012-02;
Ensure the timely submission to the Office of the Auditor of the DILG approved/endorsed GAD plan and budget pursuant to COA Circular No. 2014-001; and
Prepare and submit the annual GAD Accomplishment Report in accordance with the format and information/data prescribed under Annex E of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01 and approved by the LCE.

We reiterate our previous year’s recommendations that the CPDO and the City GFP System:
Prioritize the institution of the LGU GAD Database by setting  up the appropriate systems and mechanisms that will ensure the generation, processing, review, and updating of sex-disaggregated data and will serve as basis in performance-based and gender-responsive planning,  budgeting, programming and policy formulation in accordance with Section 36 of MCW and Item No. 4.1.A. and 4.B  of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC 2013-01;
Exhaust all available resources for the collection and processing of data requirements of the GAD Database. The absence of certain data requirements should not hinder the development of the database; and
Ensure the proper maintenance of the City’s GAD Database once established.

We recommended that the Management:
Require the City Treasurer and the City Accountant to cause the timely remittance to the BIR of the taxes withheld by the City pursuant to the above-cited Revenue Regulations;
Ensure that the amount of remittances shall equal to the amount withheld monthly; and
Require the City Accountant to monitor, review and ensure that all financial transactions regarding the Due to BIR account are properly taken up in the books.

Management comments/action
The CDRRMO Head commented  that the goods that were given to the Navotas Anti-Drug Abuse Council (NADAC) was properly communicated with CSWD for their approval and was not done arbitrarily by DRRMO who is also acting as special assistant to the NADAC Chairman.
There were delays in the delivery of stockpiles procured in CYs 2017 and 2018 for LDRRM amounting to P1.500 million and P0.890 million, respectively, however, no liquidated damages were charged against the suppliers, contrary to Section 68 Rule XXII of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.
Rule XXII of the Revised IRR of  RA No. 9184, otherwise known as the Government Procurement Reform Act, provides for the liability of the contractor in case of breach of contract. Section 68 thereof pertain to liquidated damages, quoted as follows:
“All contracts executed in accordance with the Act and this IRR shall contain a provision on liquidated damages which shall be payable by the contractor in case of breach thereof. For the procurement of goods, infrastructure projects and consulting services, the amount of liquidated damages shall be at least equal to one-tenth of one percent (0.1%) of the cost of the unperformed portion for every day of delay.  Once the cumulative amount of liquidated damages reaches ten percent (10%) of the amount of the contract, the procuring entity shall rescind the contract, without prejudice to other courses of action and remedies open to it.”
The City of Navotas  procured goods for stock piling which were charged against the LDRRM Fund during CYs 2017 and 2018 amounting to P1,499,000 and P891,119.20, respectively, which were received and paid during CY 2018. The delivery terms were both “not more than 90 days” or should be delivered on or before August 20, 2017 and September 20, 2018, respectively. However, the goods were not delivered on the expected dates as agreed upon in the Purchase Order, but no liquidated damages were charged to the suppliers, contrary to the above quoted provision. Based on the two POs the  liquidated damages totals  P262,454.99.
We recommended that Management instruct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to impose and collect liquidated damages on suppliers who failed to make complete delivery within the stipulated period.

Updating and/or revision of the City’s 10-year SWM Plan is not yet undertaken as provided for in  Sections 12.7 and 16 of RA 9003.
Section 16 of RA No. 9003 requires the City SWM Board to prepare their respective 10-year SWM Plans (SWMP) consistent with the National SWM Framework and in accordance with the provisions of  the Act and the policies set by the National Solid Waste Management Commission (NSWMC) which will approve such plan.
In this connection, the City Environment and Natural Resources Office (CENRO) submitted the City’s  10-year SWM Plan (2015-2024) to the NSWMC which was approved on November 25, 2015 under  its Resolution 
No. 210, series of 2015.
Review and updating of the City’s SWM Plan by the City SWM Board are  mandated under Section 12.7 and 16 of RA No. 9003 and Sec. 4, Rule VI of its IRR every one to two years or as the need arises  for purposes of ensuring its sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance in relation to local and international developments in the field of solid waste management.  It is also included in Chapter 1 of the City SWM Plan that the plan must be dynamic in order to be flexible and adaptable to cope with progress and introduction of new technologies, hence, it must be reviewed every year and amended accordingly or whenever necessary.
Considering the aforementioned observations and the  increase in the number of barangays in CY 2018 from 14 Barangays  to 18 Barangays and the 
non-compliance of the City from the MRF to the provisional MRS, the City’s 10-year SWM Plan needs to be reviewed and updated in accordance with RA 9003 and its IRR.
We recommended that the City SWM Board  revisit the City’s 10-year SWM Plan and incorporate therein all updates or  changes  for  review and amendments  and submission to the NSWMC for concurrence or approval.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Government thru the CHO adopt effective measures to integrate the needs requirements under the TSeKaP in the administration of PFPR. This will help to make certain that the items subject of procurement to be charged against the PFPR will be based on well-defined and priority needs of the entitled members and in accordance with the guidelines set by the PHIC.
